stem:
A 9-year-old boy is brought to your emergency department after 3 weeks of polyuria, polydipsia, and 4 kg weight loss. His mother says 'he's been drinking from the tap at night and wet the bed twice last week, which he never does'. He has had no vomiting, no abdominal pain, and is eating normally. He has had no recent viral illness and no family history of autoimmune disease.

On examination he is alert, well-perfused, with moist mucous membranes and a capillary refill under 2 seconds. There is no Kussmaul breathing and his breath is not ketotic. Bedside finger-prick glucose is 22 mmol/L. Capillary blood ketones measure 0.6 mmol/L (under 0.6 normal). Venous blood gas shows pH 7.36 (7.35-7.45) and bicarbonate 22 mmol/L (22-26). Urine dipstick shows glucose 3+, ketones trace, no nitrites or leukocytes.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 96, BP 108/68 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert, well-perfused, mucous membranes moist, no Kussmaul breathing, no abdominal tenderness, weight tracking 50th centile
data_table.Investigations: Capillary glucose 22 mmol/L, capillary ketones 0.6 mmol/L, venous pH 7.36, bicarbonate 22 mmol/L, urine glucose 3+ ketones trace

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit for intravenous 0.9% sodium chloride bolus and commence a fixed-rate insulin infusion
options.rationale: Intravenous fluids and an insulin infusion are reserved for diabetic ketoacidosis (pH under 7.3 or bicarbonate under 15 mmol/L) or significant dehydration; this child is normoglycaemic-range for new-onset diabetes without acidosis. (Source: APEG Type 1 Diabetes Handbook)

options.text: Admit under the paediatric endocrinology team for subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin and structured education
options.rationale: New-onset paediatric type 1 diabetes without ketoacidosis is admitted for subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin initiation, structured family education, and arrangement of outpatient diabetes team follow-up. RCH and APEG protocols use a multi-day inpatient education pathway before discharge. (Source: APEG Type 1 Diabetes Handbook; RCH CPG: Diabetes mellitus)

options.text: Arrange outpatient oral glucose tolerance testing and dietary review with the general practitioner in 1 week
options.rationale: OGTT is unnecessary when a child has classical symptoms and a random glucose over 11.1 mmol/L; outpatient management without insulin risks rapid progression to ketoacidosis. (Source: APEG Type 1 Diabetes Handbook)

options.text: Commence empirical oral metformin and lifestyle measures pending islet autoantibody and C-peptide results
options.rationale: Metformin is the first agent for paediatric type 2 diabetes; this child has the type 1 phenotype (rapid weight loss, classical symptoms over weeks) and requires insulin. (Source: APEG Type 1 Diabetes Handbook)

options.text: Discharge with a glucose meter, finger-prick instructions, and a written safety-net to return if vomiting
options.rationale: Discharge without insulin or formal education is unsafe in newly diagnosed type 1 diabetes; vomiting after discharge would already represent decompensation. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetes mellitus)

explanation.summary: New-onset paediatric type 1 diabetes without acidosis is managed by admission for subcutaneous insulin initiation and structured family education over several days. Acidosis (pH under 7.3 or bicarbonate under 15 mmol/L) defines DKA and triggers a separate fluid and infusion pathway. Diagnosis rests on random glucose over 11.1 mmol/L with symptoms; OGTT is not required when symptomatic. Islet autoantibody and C-peptide testing supports type but does not delay insulin start.
key_points: DKA threshold: pH under 7.3 OR bicarbonate under 15 mmol/L
Symptomatic plus random glucose over 11.1 mmol/L confirms diabetes - no OGTT
Subcutaneous basal-bolus initiation in absence of acidosis
Family education during admission is part of the standard pathway
explanation.pearls: Diabetic ketoacidosis fluid replacement in children uses 0.9% sodium chloride at a maintenance rate calculated over 48 hours, never as repeated large boluses, to reduce cerebral oedema risk.

stem:
A 7-year-old girl with known asthma is brought to your emergency department with 6 hours of worsening shortness of breath and wheeze. Her mother says 'she usually uses her blue puffer twice a week, but today she's needed it every 20 minutes and it's not really helping'. She has had a runny nose for 3 days. She is on a daily preventer (low-dose inhaled corticosteroid).

On arrival she is talking in short phrases, sitting upright, with audible wheeze and visible accessory muscle use. There is no cyanosis and she remains alert. She is given salbutamol 12 puffs by spacer and oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg. After three back-to-back salbutamol cycles, her tachypnoea has improved partially but the work of breathing remains moderate. There is good air entry bilaterally with persistent expiratory wheeze. Mum reports she had one prior admission for asthma 2 years ago and no history of intubation.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 140, BP 102/64 mmHg, RR 36, SpO2 92% on room air rising to 96% on supplemental oxygen, temperature 37.0 °C
data_table.Examination: Sitting upright, talking in short phrases, suprasternal and subcostal recession, widespread expiratory wheeze with good air entry, no silent chest, alert
data_table.Investigations: Bedside finger-prick glucose 5.4 mmol/L, capillary blood gas not yet performed; chest X-ray clear
data_table.Medications: Daily inhaled corticosteroid (preventer); salbutamol 12 puffs by spacer x 3 cycles and oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg given so far

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Intubate immediately and ventilate for impending respiratory failure with permissive hypercapnia
options.rationale: Intubation is reserved for exhaustion, silent chest, falling oxygen saturation, or altered consciousness; this child remains alert with good air entry and improving tachypnoea. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma)

options.text: Add nebulised ipratropium bromide 250 mcg with the next two salbutamol doses given back-to-back
options.rationale: Severe asthma not fully responsive to initial salbutamol plus oral steroid receives nebulised ipratropium bromide 250 mcg added to the first three back-to-back salbutamol doses; this is the AU paediatric step-up at this severity. Intravenous magnesium sulfate is the next escalation if she fails. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma; National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook)
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Commence an intravenous aminophylline loading dose with cardiac monitoring on the resuscitation bay
options.rationale: Aminophylline is a later step in life-threatening asthma after magnesium has failed; it has a narrow therapeutic window and significant adverse effects. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma)

options.text: Discontinue inhaled salbutamol and start a subcutaneous adrenaline 1:1000 0.01 mg/kg injection
options.rationale: Subcutaneous adrenaline is used for anaphylactic bronchospasm or as a temporising measure when inhaled therapy cannot be delivered; nebulised therapy is being delivered effectively here. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma)

options.text: Discharge home with a written asthma action plan and review by general practitioner in 24 hours
options.rationale: Children requiring continuous bronchodilator and accessory muscle use require ongoing inpatient observation; discharge is unsafe until the salbutamol interval has stretched to at least 3 hours. (Source: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook)
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

explanation.summary: Acute paediatric asthma is graded mild, moderate, severe, or life-threatening by work of breathing, air entry, oxygen saturation, and conscious state. Severe disease starts with back-to-back salbutamol, oral prednisolone, and oxygen as needed; persistent severity adds nebulised ipratropium to the first three back-to-back doses. Failure to respond escalates to intravenous magnesium sulfate, then aminophylline. Silent chest, exhaustion, or altered conscious state mandates paediatric intensive care escalation.
key_points: Severity grades: mild, moderate, severe, life-threatening
Severe: back-to-back salbutamol plus ipratropium plus oral steroid
Failure of severe pathway: IV magnesium sulfate next
Life-threatening features: silent chest, exhaustion, altered consciousness
explanation.pearls: Nebulised ipratropium 250 mcg is added to the first three back-to-back salbutamol doses in severe asthma in children under 12, and stopped thereafter to avoid no incremental benefit.

sources.label: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

stem:
A 13-year-old boy is brought to your general practice clinic with 6 weeks of right knee and thigh pain and a limp that has worsened over the past week. His mother says 'he stopped running around because his leg hurts and he keeps turning his foot out when he walks'. He has had no fever, no trauma, and no recent illness. He is at the 95th centile for weight and 50th for height.

On examination he is afebrile. He prefers to lie with the right leg externally rotated. Passive flexion of the right hip causes obligate external rotation and produces pain. Internal rotation of the right hip is limited to 5 degrees. Knee examination is unremarkable. He can weight-bear but limps with an out-toed gait. Lower limb neurovascular examination is normal. Plain frog-lateral pelvic X-ray shows a posterior and medial slip of the right capital femoral epiphysis on the metaphysis; the contralateral side is normal.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88, BP 116/68 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Obligate external rotation on passive hip flexion, restricted internal rotation right hip, knee unremarkable, neurovascular intact
data_table.Investigations: Plain pelvis X-ray with frog-lateral views: posterior and medial slip of right capital femoral epiphysis; left hip normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Strict non-weight-bearing with crutches and urgent paediatric orthopaedic referral for surgical pinning
options.rationale: Slipped capital femoral epiphysis is a paediatric orthopaedic emergency. The patient is made non-weight-bearing immediately to reduce the risk of slip progression and avascular necrosis, and admitted for urgent in-situ percutaneous pinning. Bilateral prophylactic pinning is considered in selected high-risk patients but the contralateral side is usually monitored. (Source: AOA Australian Orthopaedic Association SCFE Position)
source_refs: AOA Australian Orthopaedic Association SCFE Position

options.text: Outpatient referral to physiotherapy for hip range of motion strengthening and a 6-week activity plan
options.rationale: Physiotherapy in active SCFE risks acute-on-chronic progression of the slip; this is a surgical emergency. (Source: AOA Australian Orthopaedic Association SCFE Position)
source_refs: AOA Australian Orthopaedic Association SCFE Position

options.text: Magnetic resonance imaging of the right hip before discussing definitive orthopaedic management options
options.rationale: Diagnosis has been made on plain X-ray with frog-lateral views; MRI is reserved for pre-slip suspicion with normal X-rays. Imaging delay risks progression. (Source: AOA Australian Orthopaedic Association SCFE Position)
source_refs: AOA Australian Orthopaedic Association SCFE Position

options.text: Prescribe oral ibuprofen analgesia for activity-related pain and routine review by general practitioner in 1 week
options.rationale: Analgesia masks pain that protects against further slip; routine review in a week is unacceptable for an emergency. (Source: AOA Australian Orthopaedic Association SCFE Position)
source_refs: AOA Australian Orthopaedic Association SCFE Position

options.text: Investigate for transient synovitis with hip ultrasound and a course of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory cover
options.rationale: Transient synovitis presents acutely in younger children with a recent viral illness and an irritable hip on examination; the X-ray diagnosis here is SCFE. (Source: RCH CPG: Limping or non-weight-bearing child)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Limping or non-weight-bearing child

explanation.summary: Slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE) is the commonest hip disorder in adolescents, particularly overweight boys aged 10-15 years. Knee or thigh pain with a limp is the most common presentation; obligate external rotation on hip flexion is the classic examination sign. Diagnosis is by plain pelvis X-ray with frog-lateral views demonstrating slip; MRI detects pre-slip with normal X-rays. Treatment is urgent in-situ percutaneous pinning with strict non-weight-bearing in the interim to prevent acute-on-chronic slip and avascular necrosis.
key_points: SCFE is a paediatric orthopaedic emergency - immediate non-weight-bearing
Obligate external rotation on passive hip flexion is the classic sign
Plain pelvis with frog-lateral views is the diagnostic test
Avascular necrosis risk rises with delay and weight-bearing
explanation.pearls: Knee or thigh pain in an adolescent with no clear knee pathology should prompt a hip examination; SCFE can present as referred knee pain without any hip complaint.

sources.label: AOA Australian Orthopaedic Association SCFE Position
RCH CPG: Limping or non-weight-bearing child

subtopic_detail: Brief resolved unexplained event risk stratification
stem:
A 5-week-old, term, exclusively breastfed boy is brought to your emergency department after a 30-second episode during feeding in which his mother says 'he went floppy and his face turned a bit blue around the lips, then he came right after I patted his back'. He is now alert and feeding normally. He was born at 39+5 weeks gestation by uncomplicated vaginal birth, with no neonatal nursery admission.

There have been no other episodes and no recent illness. He has no fever and no cough. There is no family history of sudden unexpected death in infancy. Examination is entirely normal: tone, fontanelle, chest auscultation, abdominal examination, hip examination, oxygen saturation in room air, and capillary refill are all normal. Bedside finger-prick glucose is 4.8 mmol/L. He has fed twice in the department and remained well-perfused with stable observations. His mother asks 'do we get to go home?'.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 140, BP 78/48 mmHg, RR 42, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert, well-perfused, tone normal, fontanelle soft, chest clear, abdomen soft, hips stable, capillary refill under 2 seconds
data_table.Investigations: Finger-prick glucose 4.8 mmol/L; no other tests indicated by guideline at this point

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit for cardiorespiratory monitoring, send pertussis PCR, electrocardiogram, and electroencephalogram during admission
options.rationale: This is a low-risk BRUE (age over 60 days when assessed, term, no concerning features, normal examination, single brief episode); panel testing and admission is not part of the low-risk algorithm. (Source: AAP BRUE Clinical Practice Guideline)

options.text: Discharge after shared decision-making with safety-net advice and early review by general practitioner
options.rationale: This infant is under 60 days of age, which classifies him as a higher-risk BRUE; outright discharge without observation does not meet low-risk criteria. (Source: AAP BRUE Clinical Practice Guideline)

options.text: Observe in a short-stay unit for at least 4 hours with continuous saturation and cardiorespiratory monitoring
options.rationale: Infants under 60 days are not classified as low-risk BRUE even when otherwise reassuring. A period of monitored short-stay observation, capturing repeat events and reviewing feeding, is the AU pathway, with discharge once observation is unremarkable and parental education and safety-netting are documented. (Source: RCH CPG: Brief resolved unexplained event; AAP BRUE Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Brief resolved unexplained event

options.text: Refer to gastroenterology for upper endoscopy for suspected gastro-oesophageal reflux disease workup
options.rationale: Routine gastroenterology workup is not indicated after an isolated BRUE without recurrent feeding-related events or growth failure. (Source: RCH CPG: Brief resolved unexplained event)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Brief resolved unexplained event

options.text: Order a metabolic screen, ammonia, lactate, and a head magnetic resonance imaging study urgently
options.rationale: Broad metabolic and imaging panels are reserved for higher-risk BRUEs with concerning features (recurrent events, persistent abnormal examination, family history of metabolic disease). (Source: AAP BRUE Clinical Practice Guideline)

explanation.summary: A brief resolved unexplained event (BRUE) replaces the older ALTE term. Low-risk BRUE requires age over 60 days, gestation over 32 weeks with corrected age over 45 weeks, a single brief episode under 1 minute, no cardiopulmonary resuscitation by a trained provider, and a normal examination. Low-risk BRUE allows discharge after shared decision-making; higher-risk BRUE is observed and selectively investigated. Pertussis testing, ECG, and head imaging are pursued only when targeted features (cough exposure, dysrhythmia clues, focal neurological signs) are present.
key_points: Low-risk BRUE criteria include age over 60 days
Higher-risk: short-stay observation and selective testing
Pertussis PCR if cough exposure or paroxysmal episode
ECG for any suspected arrhythmia or syncope-like features
explanation.pearls: BRUE is by definition unexplained: a clear precipitant (gastro-oesophageal reflux event observed, choking on milk, breath-holding spell) means the event is not a BRUE and is managed on its own pathway.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Brief resolved unexplained event

stem:
A 14-month-old girl is admitted to your general paediatric ward with 6 days of persistent fever to 39.4 °C. Her father says 'she's been miserable, her eyes look really pink and she's chewing her lip because it's all cracked'. There has been no diarrhoea, no cough, and no contact with anyone unwell. She was previously well; immunisations are up to date.

On examination she has bilateral non-purulent bulbar conjunctival injection, dry fissured lips, and an erythematous oropharynx without exudate. There is a 1.8 cm right anterior cervical lymph node and a fading non-vesicular trunk rash. Her hands and feet are slightly swollen and tender; she has not desquamated. The remaining examination is unremarkable. Bloods show platelets 612 x10^9/L (200-450), CRP 142 mg/L (under 5), albumin 28 g/L (35-50), ALT 92 U/L (under 35), and a urine dipstick showing leukocyte 2+ with negative culture and no nitrites.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 158, BP 102/64 mmHg, RR 30, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 39.4 °C
data_table.Examination: Bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, dry fissured lips, erythematous oropharynx, 1.8 cm right anterior cervical node, fading trunk rash, oedematous tender hands and feet
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 612 x10^9/L (200-450), CRP 142 mg/L (under 5), albumin 28 g/L (35-50), ALT 92 U/L (under 35), urine leukocyte 2+ culture negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg over 10-12 hours plus high-dose aspirin and arrange echocardiography
options.rationale: Incomplete Kawasaki disease is diagnosed when fever has lasted at least 5 days with 2-3 of the 5 principal features and supportive laboratory abnormalities (CRP over 30, platelets over 450, albumin under 30, ALT raised, sterile pyuria). IVIG 2 g/kg with aspirin started before day 10 reduces coronary artery aneurysm risk. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Scientific Statement; RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Initiate empirical intravenous flucloxacillin and ceftriaxone for suspected bacterial cervical lymphadenitis
options.rationale: Cervical adenopathy in Kawasaki is part of the syndrome and not a bacterial node; isolated antibiotic therapy delays IVIG and does not prevent aneurysm. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Wait until the 10-day mark of fever to commence intravenous immunoglobulin in the routine treatment window
options.rationale: IVIG given as soon as the diagnosis is established (and ideally before day 10) reduces aneurysm risk; waiting until day 10 worsens cardiac outcome. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Scientific Statement)

options.text: Treat with oral prednisolone alone for suspected systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis with quotidian fever
options.rationale: Systemic JIA typically has a quotidian fever with an evanescent rash and arthritis; conjunctivitis, lip changes, and acute-phase reactant pattern point toward Kawasaki disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Continue supportive care with paracetamol and recheck inflammatory markers in 48 hours before deciding
options.rationale: Each day of untreated Kawasaki disease past day 5 of fever raises coronary aneurysm risk; supportive care alone is unacceptable. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

explanation.summary: Kawasaki disease is a paediatric vasculitis with five principal features: bilateral non-exudative conjunctivitis, polymorphous rash, oral mucosal changes (red cracked lips, strawberry tongue), peripheral changes (oedema, erythema, desquamation), and unilateral cervical adenopathy (over 1.5 cm). Complete disease requires fever at least 5 days plus four of five features; incomplete requires fever plus 2-3 features supported by laboratory abnormalities. The treatment is IVIG 2 g/kg plus aspirin within the first 10 days to prevent coronary artery aneurysms. Baseline echocardiography is performed.
key_points: Complete: fever >=5 days plus 4 of 5 features
Incomplete: fever plus 2-3 features plus supportive labs
IVIG before day 10 reduces aneurysm risk
Baseline echocardiography to detect early coronary changes
explanation.pearls: Incomplete Kawasaki disease in infants under 12 months is the highest aneurysm risk group; treat empirically when the supportive lab pattern fits even if features are incomplete.

stem:
A 3-year-old girl is brought to your emergency department 90 minutes after falling from a sofa onto a carpeted floor. Her mother says 'she cried straight away, didn't pass out, vomited once on the way in, and now she just wants to sleep on me'. The fall height was about 80 cm. She has had no further vomiting and is consolable when alert. She has had no fever or recent illness.

On examination she is GCS 15 when stimulated, drowsy at rest but easily roused with appropriate interaction. There is a 3 cm boggy parietal haematoma on the right and no other scalp injury. Fontanelles are closed. Pupils are equal and reactive at 3 mm bilaterally. Tympanic membranes are intact, no haemotympanum, and no CSF rhinorrhoea. No focal neurological deficit on screening. The remaining systemic examination is unremarkable. She has not had any anti-emetic.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 112, BP 96/60 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: GCS 15, drowsy at rest, easily roused, right parietal 3 cm boggy haematoma, pupils 3 mm bilaterally reactive, no focal deficit, tympanic membranes intact
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 5.1 mmol/L, no other investigations performed yet

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home with written head injury safety-net advice and review by general practitioner in 48 hours
options.rationale: The combination of age under 2 to 3 with a parietal non-frontal scalp haematoma and observed lethargy fails the PECARN low-risk criteria for under-2s and requires observation or imaging. (Source: PECARN Paediatric Head Injury Rule)

options.text: Observe in the emergency department for at least 4-6 hours with serial neurological examinations
options.rationale: Under PECARN intermediate-risk criteria (non-frontal scalp haematoma in under 2, lethargy, single vomit) the choice is between observation and imaging, with parental input. A period of observation with serial GCS, pupil checks, and trend of conscious state is the AU first-line approach in a stable child with no focal signs. CT is the next step if deterioration or persistent symptoms occur. (Source: RCH CPG: Head injury; PECARN Paediatric Head Injury Rule)

options.text: Order a non-contrast computed tomography head scan immediately for the high-risk features described
options.rationale: Immediate CT is reserved for high-risk PECARN features (GCS under 15 at 2 hours, suspected open or depressed skull fracture, signs of basal skull fracture, suspected non-accidental injury); observation is acceptable for intermediate-risk children. (Source: PECARN Paediatric Head Injury Rule)

options.text: Arrange skull X-ray series to look for a linear or depressed fracture before further decision-making
options.rationale: Plain skull X-ray has been superseded by clinical decision rules; it neither rules in nor out clinically important traumatic brain injury reliably. (Source: RCH CPG: Head injury)

options.text: Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain on the next available outpatient slot to avoid radiation exposure
options.rationale: MRI is the second-line imaging modality for paediatric head injury (typically follow-up) and is impractical acutely without anaesthesia; CT is the acute test where imaging is required. (Source: RCH CPG: Head injury)

explanation.summary: PECARN stratifies paediatric blunt head injury into high, intermediate, and low risk. High-risk features mandate CT; low-risk allows discharge. Intermediate risk allows observation versus CT with parental input. In children under 2, an isolated non-frontal scalp haematoma is intermediate. Observation with serial examination spares unnecessary radiation in an at-risk paediatric brain. Children with high-risk features (GCS under 15 at 2 hours, palpable depressed fracture, basal skull fracture signs, non-accidental injury concerns) are imaged.
key_points: PECARN high-risk: GCS under 15 at 2 hours, suspected depressed or basal skull fracture, NAI
Intermediate-risk under 2: non-frontal scalp haematoma is a positive predictor
Observation with serial examination is acceptable in intermediate risk
CT not MRI for acute decision-making
explanation.pearls: In children under 2, an isolated non-frontal scalp haematoma is an intermediate-risk PECARN feature; a frontal haematoma in an otherwise well child does not elevate risk.

subtopic_detail: Pulse oximetry screening for critical congenital heart disease
stem:
You are the resident on the postnatal ward. A term newborn boy at 28 hours of age is screened with the routine pulse oximetry protocol. The midwife reports the readings to you. He is feeding, has had a wet nappy, and his mother says 'he looks pink to me'. His mother had unremarkable antenatal scans including a 20-week morphology scan. There is no family history of congenital heart disease.

On examination he is pink centrally and well-perfused, with normal tone. Cardiac auscultation reveals normal first and second heart sounds, no murmur, and femoral pulses are present and synchronous. Chest auscultation is clear. Capillary refill is under 2 seconds and the abdomen is soft. The four-limb blood pressure is unremarkable. Pre-ductal (right hand) SpO2 is 95% and post-ductal (right foot) SpO2 is 92% on three readings each, separated by 2 minutes; the difference is consistent across readings.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 142, BP 72/44 mmHg, RR 44, pre-ductal SpO2 95%, post-ductal SpO2 92%, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Pink centrally, well-perfused, soft fontanelle, no murmur, normal heart sounds, synchronous femoral pulses, clear chest, soft abdomen
data_table.Investigations: Pulse oximetry protocol over 6 minutes shows persistent pre-post differential 3% with both feet under 95%

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure the mother, repeat pulse oximetry in 24 hours, and continue routine ward observations meanwhile
options.rationale: Persistent pre-post differential under 95% on the AU pulse oximetry algorithm at this stage triggers urgent echocardiography rather than delayed re-screening. (Source: NSW Health Pulse Oximetry Screening Newborn Guideline)
source_refs: NSW Health Pulse Oximetry Screening Newborn Guideline

options.text: Refer urgently for echocardiography and discuss with the on-call paediatric cardiology service today
options.rationale: The AU pulse oximetry screening algorithm fails when either limb is under 90%, or both limbs under 95%, or a persistent pre-post differential of more than 3%; failure prompts urgent echocardiography to exclude critical congenital heart disease (duct-dependent lesions). Prostaglandin E1 is held in reserve pending the result. (Source: NSW Health Pulse Oximetry Screening Newborn Guideline; RCH CPG: Critical congenital heart disease)
source_refs: NSW Health Pulse Oximetry Screening Newborn Guideline
RCH CPG: Critical congenital heart disease

options.text: Commence empirical prostaglandin E1 infusion immediately before any further imaging investigation
options.rationale: Empirical prostaglandin is reserved for clinically unwell duct-dependent neonates; the screen has identified an asymptomatic child for whom prompt echocardiography is the appropriate step. (Source: RCH CPG: Critical congenital heart disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Critical congenital heart disease

options.text: Discharge home with general practitioner follow-up and a 1-week outpatient cardiology clinic appointment
options.rationale: Discharge before imaging in a baby with a positive screen is unsafe; duct closure (which usually occurs in the first week) can precipitate decompensation of a duct-dependent lesion. (Source: NSW Health Pulse Oximetry Screening Newborn Guideline)
source_refs: NSW Health Pulse Oximetry Screening Newborn Guideline

options.text: Order arterial blood gas first and only proceed to echocardiography if metabolic acidosis is documented
options.rationale: Echocardiography is the definitive imaging step; awaiting blood gas changes delays diagnosis and may miss asymptomatic anatomic disease before deterioration. (Source: RCH CPG: Critical congenital heart disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Critical congenital heart disease

explanation.summary: Australian neonatal pulse oximetry screening detects critical congenital heart disease (CCHD) at 24-48 hours of age, before duct closure typically unmasks duct-dependent lesions. The protocol measures pre-ductal (right hand) and post-ductal (foot) saturation. A fail occurs with either limb under 90%, both limbs under 95% on three readings 1 hour apart, or a persistent pre-post differential of more than 3%. Failed screen mandates urgent echocardiography; prostaglandin E1 is reserved for clinical decompensation pending the result.
key_points: Screen at 24-48 hours of age, before discharge
Fail thresholds: either limb under 90%, both under 95%, or pre-post differential over 3%
Failed screen: urgent echocardiography, not repeat screening
Prostaglandin E1 reserved for clinical decompensation
explanation.pearls: Duct closure in the first week of life unmasks duct-dependent CCHD (HLHS, critical AS, severe coarctation, IAA); the pulse oximetry screen is designed to find these before that catastrophe.

sources.label: NSW Health Pulse Oximetry Screening Newborn Guideline
RCH CPG: Critical congenital heart disease

stem:
You are the paediatric registrar called to assess a previously well 4-year-old boy of Mediterranean heritage who has presented with 24 hours of fatigue, pallor, and dark coloured urine. His mother says 'his wee looks like Coca-Cola and the whites of his eyes have gone yellow today'. Three days ago he ate fresh fava beans at a relative's gathering and was started on oral co-trimoxazole for an ear infection. He has no fever now and no abdominal pain.

On examination he is alert, pale, with scleral icterus. Cardiovascular examination shows a quiet flow murmur at the left sternal edge; the spleen tip is palpable but not enlarged. There is no lymphadenopathy and no petechiae. Bloods show haemoglobin 72 g/L (115-150), reticulocytes 240 x10^9/L (50-100), unconjugated bilirubin 68 micromol/L (under 17), LDH 1480 U/L (under 250), haptoglobin under 0.1 g/L (0.3-2.0), direct antiglobulin test negative. A blood film reports bite cells and Heinz bodies. He has no known prior haemolytic episodes.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 128, BP 102/64 mmHg, RR 26, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert, pale, scleral icterus, soft systolic flow murmur, spleen tip just palpable, no lymphadenopathy, no purpura
data_table.Investigations: Haemoglobin 72 g/L (115-150), reticulocytes 240 x10^9/L (50-100), unconjugated bilirubin 68 micromol/L (under 17), LDH 1480 U/L (under 250), haptoglobin under 0.1 g/L (0.3-2.0), DAT negative, blood film bite cells and Heinz bodies
data_table.Medications: Oral co-trimoxazole started 3 days ago for otitis media

lead_in: What is the single best initial management step?

options.text: Withdraw co-trimoxazole, treat any persistent infection with an alternative agent, and supportive transfusion if symptomatic
options.rationale: Acute G6PD-mediated haemolysis is precipitated by an oxidative trigger (fava beans, sulfonamides such as co-trimoxazole, nitrofurantoin, primaquine, dapsone, methylene blue, rasburicase). Removal of the trigger is the foundation; red cell transfusion is reserved for symptomatic anaemia, haemodynamic compromise, or rapid haemoglobin fall. Re-introduce safe antibiotics for the otitis. (Source: HSANZ; RCH CPG: Haemolytic anaemia)

options.text: Empirical intravenous methylprednisolone pulses for warm autoimmune haemolytic anaemia presumption
options.rationale: Warm autoimmune haemolytic anaemia is DAT-positive (typically with IgG, with or without complement); this child has DAT-negative haemolysis with bite cells and Heinz bodies, characteristic of G6PD deficiency, not autoimmune disease. (Source: HSANZ)

options.text: Urgent splenectomy referral for management of compensated chronic haemolysis with hyperbilirubinaemia
options.rationale: Splenectomy is considered in hereditary spherocytosis with chronic transfusion need or symptomatic anaemia; G6PD deficiency haemolysis is episodic and trigger-related, and splenectomy is rarely useful. (Source: HSANZ)

options.text: Administer rasburicase prophylactically for tumour lysis prevention before chemotherapy assessment
options.rationale: Rasburicase is one of the agents that can precipitate severe G6PD haemolysis and should be avoided; this child has a non-malignant haemolytic picture. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Commence methylene blue infusion for any associated methaemoglobinaemia before haematology review
options.rationale: Methylene blue is also contraindicated in G6PD deficiency because it relies on NADPH-dependent reduction and itself precipitates haemolysis. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: G6PD deficiency is X-linked and most common in boys from Mediterranean, African, Middle Eastern, and South-East Asian backgrounds. Acute haemolysis is triggered by oxidative stress (fava beans, sulfonamides, nitrofurantoin, primaquine, dapsone, methylene blue, rasburicase, infection). Blood film shows bite cells, blister cells, and Heinz bodies; the direct antiglobulin test is negative. Management removes the trigger and supports with transfusion if symptomatic or haemodynamically compromised. G6PD enzyme assay during the acute haemolysis can be falsely normal because reticulocytes have higher activity; repeat 2-3 months after the episode. A familial G6PD test list and an avoid-list for future medications is essential.
key_points: Oxidative trigger list: fava beans, sulfonamides, nitrofurantoin, primaquine, dapsone, methylene blue, rasburicase
DAT-negative with bite cells and Heinz bodies
Repeat enzyme assay 2-3 months post-event - acute assay can be falsely normal
Family screening and a written avoid-medication list
explanation.pearls: Methylene blue and rasburicase are both contraindicated in G6PD deficiency despite their utility elsewhere; check G6PD status before either drug in at-risk populations.

stem:
A 28-year-old primigravida at 34+4 weeks gestation is admitted to your delivery suite with a 1-day history of frontal headache and right upper quadrant pain. Her midwife says 'her blood pressure has been creeping up and the protein-creatinine ratio came back high'. She has had three booking visits with normal blood pressure and a normal 20-week morphology scan. She has no history of seizures and no chronic hypertension.

On examination she is alert, GCS 15, with normal cranial nerves. Reflexes are brisk symmetrically with 2 beats of clonus at both ankles. Abdomen is gravid with mild right upper quadrant tenderness and a single live foetus on cardiotocography with a reassuring trace. Her vital signs include BP 168/112 mmHg confirmed on repeat manual reading 15 minutes apart. Bloods show platelets 122 x10^9/L (150-450), ALT 96 U/L (under 35), creatinine 88 micromol/L (45-90), urate 0.42 mmol/L (0.16-0.36), and urine protein-to-creatinine ratio 78 mg/mmol (under 30).

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 92, BP 168/112 mmHg confirmed on repeat manual reading, RR 18, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C, foetal HR 142 bpm reassuring
data_table.Examination: Alert, GCS 15, brisk reflexes with 2 beats clonus bilaterally, RUQ tenderness, gravid abdomen, single foetus on CTG
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 122 x10^9/L (150-450), ALT 96 U/L (under 35), creatinine 88 micromol/L (45-90), urate 0.42 mmol/L (0.16-0.36), urine PCR 78 mg/mmol (under 30)
data_table.Medications: Nil regular antenatally; intravenous labetalol bolus 20 mg given prior to your review

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Commence intravenous magnesium sulfate loading 4 g over 20 minutes then 1 g per hour as eclampsia prophylaxis
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia (severe-range BP plus end-organ involvement) requires magnesium sulfate seizure prophylaxis: 4 g IV loading over 20 minutes followed by 1 g/hour infusion. Magnesium is continued for 24 hours after delivery or after the last seizure. Calcium gluconate is the antidote for toxicity. (Source: RANZCOG; SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Add oral nifedipine immediate release for ongoing blood pressure control without seizure prophylaxis cover
options.rationale: Antihypertensive therapy controls severe BP but does not reduce seizure risk; magnesium prophylaxis is indicated in severe disease. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Commence intravenous phenytoin loading dose for seizure prophylaxis instead of magnesium sulfate
options.rationale: Phenytoin is inferior to magnesium for eclampsia seizure prevention; magnesium is the AU and international first-line. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Hold further therapy pending repeat blood pressure in 30 minutes and bedside ultrasound for foetal wellbeing
options.rationale: BP is already confirmed severe-range on repeat measurement; further delay risks eclampsia and acute hypertensive complications. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Administer intramuscular betamethasone alone for foetal lung maturation and arrange transfer to tertiary unit
options.rationale: Antenatal corticosteroids are given for delivery anticipated within 7 days at 34 weeks or earlier, but magnesium prophylaxis is concurrent and not deferred for steroids. (Source: RANZCOG)

explanation.summary: Pre-eclampsia is new-onset hypertension at or after 20 weeks gestation with end-organ dysfunction (renal, hepatic, neurological, haematological, foetal growth restriction, placental). Severe features include BP at or above 160/110 mmHg, severe headache, visual disturbance, epigastric pain, pulmonary oedema, hepatocellular dysfunction, thrombocytopaenia, renal injury, or eclamptic seizure. Magnesium sulfate 4 g IV load then 1 g/hour reduces seizure risk by half and is the AU first-line. Antihypertensive control targets BP under 160/110 mmHg without overshoot to preserve placental perfusion.
key_points: Severe features include BP >=160/110 mmHg confirmed on repeat
Magnesium sulfate 4 g IV load then 1 g/hour for prophylaxis
Continue magnesium for 24 hours after delivery or last seizure
Calcium gluconate is the antidote for magnesium toxicity
explanation.pearls: Magnesium toxicity progression: loss of patellar reflex around 4 mmol/L, respiratory depression at 6 mmol/L, cardiac arrest above 12 mmol/L; calcium gluconate 1 g IV is the antidote.

stem:
A 32-year-old G3P3 has just had a normal vaginal birth at 39+5 weeks gestation. The placenta delivered intact 12 minutes ago and the midwife says 'the uterus feels boggy and there's a steady fresh red trickle of blood'. Estimated blood loss is now 800 mL and continuing. Active management of the third stage was used with intramuscular oxytocin 10 IU.

On examination her airway is patent, she is talking but slightly anxious, and her conjunctivae are pink. The fundus is palpable above the umbilicus and not contracted. The vulva and lower vagina are inspected and there are no genital tract lacerations needing suture; the placenta is intact when re-examined. Bedside coagulation testing has been sent and is pending. Bimanual uterine massage has been initiated and the bladder has been emptied with a urinary catheter that has just been inserted. Intravenous access is established with a 16 G cannula running 0.9% sodium chloride.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 108 regular, BP 102/64 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Boggy poorly contracted uterus above the umbilicus, no perineal laceration, placenta inspected and intact, fresh red ongoing trickle, catheter inserted, 16 G IV access
data_table.Investigations: FBC, coags, fibrinogen, group and hold sent; bedside lactate 2.1 mmol/L (under 2.0)
data_table.Medications: Active third stage included intramuscular oxytocin 10 IU

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Administer intravenous oxytocin 5 IU slowly then commence an oxytocin infusion 40 IU in 1000 mL over 4 hours
options.rationale: Uterine atony is the most common cause of primary postpartum haemorrhage. First-line pharmacological treatment after concurrent uterine massage and bladder emptying is intravenous oxytocin 5 IU slowly followed by an oxytocin infusion; ergometrine, carboprost, and intramyometrial carboprost are the next steps if atony persists. Tranexamic acid is given in parallel within 3 hours of onset. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Statement; AMH)

options.text: Inject intramuscular ergometrine 250 mcg without prior assessment of blood pressure or pre-eclampsia history
options.rationale: Ergometrine is a second-line uterotonic and is contraindicated in hypertension or pre-eclampsia; oxytocin is preferred first. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Administer rectal misoprostol 800 mcg as the only uterotonic without intravenous oxytocin or massage
options.rationale: Misoprostol is a useful adjunct, particularly in low-resource settings, but is not the AU first-line when intravenous oxytocin is available. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Statement)

options.text: Proceed directly to balloon tamponade with a Bakri balloon as the first step in pharmacological management
options.rationale: Balloon tamponade is a non-pharmacological adjunct used after first-line uterotonics fail to control bleeding; uterotonic escalation precedes it. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Statement)

options.text: Withhold further uterotonics and observe for spontaneous uterine contraction over the next 15 minutes
options.rationale: Ongoing bleeding with uterine atony requires escalation; observation worsens haemodynamic compromise. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Statement)

explanation.summary: Primary postpartum haemorrhage is blood loss greater than 500 mL after vaginal birth or greater than 1000 mL after caesarean within 24 hours; the 4 Ts framework identifies tone, trauma, tissue, and thrombin causes. Atony is the commonest cause. Management is concurrent (call for help, two large-bore IVs, FBC/coags/fibrinogen/group and hold/crossmatch, massage, empty bladder, uterotonic escalation, tranexamic acid within 3 hours). The uterotonic ladder is oxytocin then ergometrine (if no hypertension) then carboprost (if no asthma) then intrauterine balloon tamponade then surgical interventions. Tranexamic acid is given regardless of the immediate cause within the 3-hour window.
key_points: 4 Ts: tone, trauma, tissue, thrombin
Uterotonic ladder: oxytocin -> ergometrine (no hypertension) -> carboprost (no asthma)
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hours of onset
Balloon tamponade after uterotonic failure
explanation.pearls: Carboprost (PGF2alpha) 250 mcg intramuscular every 15 minutes up to 8 doses is reserved for atony refractory to oxytocin and ergometrine; it is contraindicated in asthma.
